coredata.dc:title: Sophorajaponica L. flower mediated carbon dots with nitrogen and sulfur co-doped as a sensitive fluorescent probe for amoxicillin detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Xin-Ran
Dong, Yu-Ming
Chen, Xin-Yue
Chen, Juan
coredata.dc:description: This article first reported the green synthesis of N, S co-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N, S-CDs-Sop) and sought to establish the fluorescence detection system for amoxicillin (AMX). By using Sophorajaponica L. flower as the green precursor and dl -homocystine as the co-dopant, N, S-CDs-Sop were successfully prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal method, exhibiting good water solubility and excellent photoluminescence. It was revealed that the surface of N, S-CDs-Sop was abundant in amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups after being characterized by a variety of techniques. When Fe3+ was added, Fe3+ could be complexed with N, S-CDs-Sop to from N, S-CDs-Sop-Fe3+ chelation leading to a significant static quenching of fluorescence. However, when N, S-CDs-Sop, Fe3+ and AMX coexisted, AMX would coordinate with Fe3+ and form the strong chelate due to the favorable chemical structure, resulting in the rapid fluorescence recovery. Such a fast, simple and sensitive fluorescence “off–on” strategy with a low LOD and a relatively wide range was successfully applied to the detection of AMX, which is closely correlated with human health.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
N
S-CDs-Sop
Fluorescence probe
Amoxicillin

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Green synthesis of N, S-CDs-Sop, N-CDs-Sop, S-CDs-Sop and CDs-Sop
Amoxicillin detection in human plasma sample
Synthesis of N-CDs-Sop, S-CDs-Sop, N, S-CDs-Sop and CDs-Sop
Characterization of N, S-CDs-Sop and CDs-Sop
Possible quenching and recovery mechanisms of fluorescence
Determination of AMX in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Special Foundation for National Science and Technology Basic Resources of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China
Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors greatly appreciate the financial support from the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (NO. 2019M663855 ), the Special Foundation for National Science and Technology Basic Resources of China (No. 2018FY100701 ), the Key Program of Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province, China (No. 20JR10RA584 ) and the 2021 project of Gansu Medical Products Administration (No. 2021GSMPA008 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs-Sop, CDs-Sop, S-CDs-Sop and N-CDs-Sop with the same dilution multiple excited at 335 nm. The inset is the photograph image of N, S-CDs-Sop (a), CDs-Sop (b), S-CDs-Sop (c) and N-CDs-Sop (d) under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation.
(A) Full XPS spectra of N, S-CDs-Sop and XPS high-resolution spectra of C 1 s (B), O 1 s (C), N 1 s (D) and S 2p (E) of N, S-CDs-Sop.
(A) Full XPS spectra of CDs-Sop and XPS high-resolution spectra of C 1 s (B), O 1 s (C) and N 1 s (D) of CDs-Sop.
FTIR spectra of the N, S-CDs-Sop (blue) and CDs-Sop (red).

TEM images of N, S-CDs-Sop (A) and CDs-Sop (B). Insets of (A) and (B) are the size distribution histograms of N, S-CDs-Sop and CDs-Sop, respectively. The zeta potential of N, S-CDs-Sop (C) and CDs-Sop (D).

(A) UV–vis absorbance spectra (blue curve), fluorescence excitation spectra (black curve) and emission spectra (red curve) of N, S-CDs-Sop. Inset of (A) is the photograph of the N, S-CDs-Sop solution under visible light (a) and 365 nm UV light (b). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of N, S-CDs-Sop at different excitation wavelengths (Ex = 300–380 nm).
The effects of (A and B) NaCl concentration, (C and D) temperature and (E) pH value on fluorescence of N, S-CDs-Sop (F0: the fluorescence intensity when the solution pH at 7).
(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of N, S-CDs-Sop with addition of some common metal ions and anions. (B) The histogram corresponds to (A) (F0: the fluorescence intensity of the blank sample).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs-Sop with the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+. (B) Relationship between the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs-Sop and concentration of Fe3+ from 0 to 100 μM.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N, S-CDs-Sop, N, S-CDs-Sop + Fe3+ and N, S-CDs-Sop + Fe3+ + AMX. The concentrations of Fe3+ and AMX are 25 μM and 100 μM, respectively. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of the N, S-CDs-Sop/Fe3+ system after the addition of different concentrations of AMX. (C) Relationship between (F-F0)/F0 and AMX concentration from 5 to 100 μM. (D) Linear relationship between (F-F0)/F0 and AMX concentration from 5 to 80 μM.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of N, S-CDs-Sop and N, S-CDs-Sop + 25 μM Fe3+. The inset is the photograph of N, S-CDs-Sop solution with addition of 500 μM Fe3+ under visible light. (B) Fluorescence decay traces of N, S-CDs-Sop, N, S-CDs-Sop + Fe3+, and N, S-CDs-Sop + Fe3+ + AMX. The concentrations of Fe3+and AMX are 25 μM and 100 μM, respectively. (C) The mechanism of quenching and recovery of fluorescence.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of the N, S-CDs-Sop/Fe3+ system with addition of some interfering substances. All the concentration of interfering substances is 100 μM. (B) The histogram corresponds to (A) (F0: the fluorescence intensity of the blank sample).

(A) The fluorescence emission spectrum of the N, S-CDs/Fe3+ system with the additionof various concentrations of AMX (Ex = 335 nm).(B) Relationship between (F-F0)/F0 and the concentration of AMX in human plasma samples from 10 to 90 μM. (C) The linear relationship of (F-F0)/F0 versus the concentration of AMX over the range from 10 to 70 μM.
Comparison of different detection methods of AMX.

head.ce:title: Sophorajaponica L. flower mediated carbon dots with nitrogen and sulfur co-doped as a sensitive fluorescent probe for amoxicillin detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730030, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730030, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This article first reported the green synthesis of N, S co-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N, S-CDs-Sop) and sought to establish the fluorescence detection system for amoxicillin (AMX). By using Sophorajaponica L. flower as the green precursor and dl -homocystine as the co-dopant, N, S-CDs-Sop were successfully prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal method, exhibiting good water solubility and excellent photoluminescence. It was revealed that the surface of N, S-CDs-Sop was abundant in amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups after being characterized by a variety of techniques. When Fe3+ was added, Fe3+ could be complexed with N, S-CDs-Sop to from N, S-CDs-Sop-Fe3+ chelation leading to a significant static quenching of fluorescence. However, when N, S-CDs-Sop, Fe3+ and AMX coexisted, AMX would coordinate with Fe3+ and form the strong chelate due to the favorable chemical structure, resulting in the rapid fluorescence recovery. Such a fast, simple and sensitive fluorescence “off–on” strategy with a low LOD and a relatively wide range was successfully applied to the detection of AMX, which is closely correlated with human health.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Amoxicillin (AMX), one of the semi-synthetic penicillin’s β-lactam antibiotics, is commonly used for treating bacterial infections caused by gram-negative and gram-positive organisms owing to its potent antibacterial activity and capacity to penetrate the cytoderm [1,2]. However, excessive accumulation of AMX owing to the improper and excessive use in daily life results in bacterial resistance and potential harm to human health [3]. Therefore, developing a novel analytical method is of vital importance to effectively measure AMX. At present, the quantitative detection methods of AMX have been developed such as liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [4], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5], electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [6], spectroscopy [7] and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [8]. Despite of high sensitivity and good accuracy, these methods suffer from the drawbacks of expensive equipment, complex operation processes and excessive usage of organic solvents [9]. Thus, it is urgent to develop an economic, simple, effective, and environmental-friendly strategy for AMX detection. Among the various analytical methods, the optical method is regarded as one of the most potent tools due to its rapidity, ease of use, simplicity, low cost, and high sensitivity [10].

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials and have attracted considerable attention owing to their advantages of tunable photoluminescence behavior, excellent fluorescence stability, low toxicity and high biocompatibility[11,12]. CDs are developing rapidly and have promising prospects in various fields such as fluorescence sensing [13], biological imaging [14], photocatalysis [15], antimicrobial activity [16] and targeted delivery [17]. The chemical methods are typically used in CDs synthesis for their simple operation and high reliability. However, the environment and public health would be seriously endangered by the overuse and improper handling of chemical agents. The green synthesis comes into being, which is considered to be a prospective method possessing the advantages of nature, nontoxicity, renewability and environmental friendliness [18]. Numerous natural products have been reported to be employed in the green synthesis of CDs such as banana juice [19], watermelon juice [20], citrus fruit peels [21], and rose flower [22]. The synthesized CDs derived from green synthetic precursors exhibit good performance in optics, and have been widely applied in the fields of analysis and detection [23]. For example, Liang et al. used CDs derived from wasted tobacco leaves as a fluorescent nanoprobe for tetracycline detection [24]. Yu et al. reported that CDs from osmanthus fragrans was employed as a fluorescent sensor for the detection of quercetin and aluminum ion [25]. Miao et al. prepared the CDs made from tomato juice for a label-free sensitive assay of carcinoembryonic antigen [26]. Natural products are determined to have the attributes of easy accessibility, low cost, and richness in a diversity of elements, providing great benefits for CDs synthesis and surface functionalization.

Due to its anti-oxidative and anti-inflammatory activities [27], the dried flower of Sophora japonica L. has been used as a kind of medicinal herb in China for a long time. The main chemical components of S. japonica flower include flavonoids, saponins, fatty acids, proteins, alkaloids and polysaccharides [28], which contribute to great medicinal and nutritional values. S.japonica flower contains many active ingredients that may contribute to not only pharmaceutical effects but also chemical reactions, however, its application in the green synthesis of CDs has not been reported yet.Thus, it is worth trying to use S. japonica flower as the green precursor for CDs synthesis, which may be an outstanding carbon source. Moreover, heteroatom dopants such as sulfur and nitrogen have been successfully employed to enhance the characteristics of CDs [29]. It is reported that after nitrogen and sulfur co-doping, CDs would be modified with a novel surface state, and exhibit good optical performance and a higher quantum yield (QY) [29]. Therefore, in this article, sulfuric acid, ammonia and dl -homocystine were selected as nitrogen, sulfur, and nitrogen-sulfur dopants respectively to enhance the photoluminescence properties of CDs.

Herein, N, S-CDs-Sop were efficiently prepared via a straightforward one-step hydrothermal treatment, in which S. japonica flower acted as the green precursor and dl -homocystine served as the nitrogen and sulfur co-dopant. The synthesized N, S-CDs-Sop had good water solubility as well as outstanding photoluminescence properties. After optimizing a series of metal irons, it was found that the addition of Fe3+ could effectively and rapidly quench the fluorescence of N, S-CDs-Sop through static quenching. However, AMX could preferentially combine with Fe3+ to form stable chelates, leading to the quick recovery of fluorescence emission. With an excitation wavelength of 335 nm, a linear equation was established between AMX concentration and fluorescence intensity, displaying great sensitivity and good linear correlation. The established system enabled the fast and accurate detection of AMX in the human plasma sample.

The S.japonica flowers were collected on the campus of Lanzhou University (Lanzhou, China). CuCl2·2H2O, Na2CO3, NaCl, AlCl3 and FeCl2·4H2O were purchased from Da Mao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). CaCl2, Pb(NO3)2, AgNO3 and BaCl2·2H2O were purchased from Beijing Hong Xing Chemical Plant (Beijing, China). MgSO4 was purchased from Beijing Shuang Huan Chemical Reagent Factory (Beijing, China). KCl was purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., ltd (Shanghai, China). FeCl3·6H2O was purchased from Beijing No. 17 Middle School Chemical Plant (Beijing, China). Glucose, l -alanine (Ala) and l -glutathione (GSH) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., ltd (Shanghai, China). Dopamine hydrochloride (Dap) was purchased from Shanghai Energy Chemical Co., ltd (Shanghai, China). l -Tryptophan (Trp) was purchased from J&K Scientific ltd (Beijing, China). Histidine (His), dl -homocystine and thiamphenicol (TAP) were purchased from Meryer Chemical Technology Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). l -Glutamic acid (Glu), dl -methionine (Met) and erythromycin (EM) were purchased from Solarbio life sciences Technology Co., ltd (Beijing, China). Benzylpenicillin potassium (PG), gentamicin (GM), and sulfadiazine (SD) were purchased from Shanghaiyuanye Bio-Technology Co., ltd (Shanghai, China). Tobramycin (TOB), tetracycline (TC) and amoxicillin (AMX) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd (Shanghai, China). All the chemical reagents were analytically pure and used without further purification. Double-deionized water (DDW, A.S. Watson, Beijing, China) was used throughout the analysis.

The fluorescent spectra were recorded by Spectro fluorophotometer (Shimadzu, RF-5301 PC, Japan). The slit width of excitation and emission light was 5 nm. Ultra-violet absorption spectra were recorded by Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer, lamda25, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded by using an FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet, Nexus 870, USA) to characterize the functional groups of CDs. The fluorescence lifetime was collected by a Steady-State & Time-Resolved Fluorescence Spectrofluorometer Spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, FLS920, US) with an integrating sphere. X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (Shimadzu, Axis Supra, Japan) was used to obtain the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra. Zeta potential results were obtained by laser dynamic scattering instrument Zeta sizer Nano 3600 (Malvern, UK). The morphology of CDs was observed by the FEI Tecnai G2TF20 instrument (Feitecnai, USA) with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthesis of N, S-CDs-Sop, N-CDs-Sop, S-CDs-Sop and CDs-Sop
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were synthesized by the one-pot hydrothermal method with S.japonica flowers for the first time here. The synthesis steps were as follows. The S.japonica flowers were thoroughly washed with distilled water to remove the solid impurities, dried and dehydrated completely in an oven at 60 °C and then ground into powder. A certain amount of S.japonica flowers powder (0.5 g) was scattered in 25 mL purified water and sonicated for 40 min. To explore the effects of different atomic doping on CDs fluorescence characteristics, 0.05 g dl -homocystine (nitrogen and sulfur source) was mixed with sonicated S.japonica flowers. The mixture was sealed into Teflon inner stainless-steel reactor, heated at 190 °C for 3 h and named N, S-CDs-Sop. Besides, the dopants were replaced with 0.4 mL of ammonia and 0.2 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid respectively, while other operations remained unchanged. The obtained CDs were named N-CDs-Sop and S-CDs-Sop. Meanwhile, only the S.japonica flower mixture participated in the hydrothermal reaction without dopants as a comparison, known as CDs-Sop.

After the products had been cooled to room temperature, the supernatants were collected, centrifuged for 20 min at 12000 rpm and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membranes several times to remove large particles.Finally, the obtained products were stored at 4 °C for further utilization.

To investigate the influence of pH on N, S-CDs-Sop fluorescence intensity, solution pH was adjusted from 2 to 13 by using HCl and NaOH at a concentration of 0.5 M, respectively. To examine the impact of temperature on the stability of fluorescence intensity, the change of fluorescence intensity was evaluated at the temperature in the range of 20 to 80 °C. Additionally, the change of fluorescence intensity was measured at NaCl concentration from 0 to 1.0 M.

The fluorescence detection of AMX was performed at room temperature as follows. In a typical experimental procedure, 335 nm was chosen as the optimum excitation wavelength and the emission wavelength was in the range of 350–655 nm. At first, 80 μL N, S-CDs-Sop suspension was diluted to 10 mL with DDW and considered as the standard solution for subsequent use. To determine the optimal quenched concentration, 1.8 mL N, S-CDs-Sop standard solution was mixed with 200 μL different concentrations of 50, 250, 500, 750, 1000 μM Fe3+ solution for 1 min and the fluorescence spectra were recorded. To examine the selectivity of N, S-CDs-Sop towards Fe3+, diverse metal ions (Al3+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Sr2+, K+, Mg2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Na+, Zn2+) and anions (SO4 2-, CO3 2–, NO3 –) with a final concentration of 100 μM were added into the standard solution to assess their effect on the fluorescence of the N, S-CDs-Sop.

For the detection of AMX, 100 μL of 250 μM Fe3+ solution and 100 μL of amoxicillin solution with a concentration range of 50 to 1000 μM were mixed completely in 30 s. Then, 1.8 mL N, S-CDs-Sop standard sample was added to the above mixture solution. After 1 min for equilibrium, the fluorescence intensity of each sample was measured.

The selectivity of the N, S-CDs-Sop/Fe3+ system towards AMX was examined by adding representative interference agents including Glucose, DAP, GSH, Trp, His, Ala, Glu, Met, PG, GM, EM, TOB, TAP, SD, TC. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CDs-Sop/Fe3+ system was also recorded. The ultimate concentration of interferents was 100 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amoxicillin detection in human plasma sample
ce:section.ce:para: The plasma sample was collected from a healthy volunteer. The venous blood was collected on an empty stomach and the plasma sample was separated. The plasma was thoroughly mixed with an equal volume of 4 % trichloroacetic acid (wt%) for 10 min. Then the treated plasma sample was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min to remove the insoluble proteins. The supernatant was diluted 40 times and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane for use [30]. Finally, a series of AMX solutions at various concentrations were prepared by treated plasma sample solution, and the following detection steps were shown in Section 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-CDs-Sop, S-CDs-Sop, N, S-CDs-Sop and CDs-Sop
ce:section.ce:para: As described in Section 2.3, four kinds of CDs were synthesized by using S.japonica flower as the green precursor with different dopants for the first time via a one-pot hydrothermal method at 190 °C for 3 h. The introduction of dopants was aimed to investigate the effects of different elements doping on the luminescence properties of CDs.

As shown in Fig. 1 , after the four kinds of obtained CDs were diluted by the same multiple, their emission spectra with the excitation wavelength at 335 nm were recorded and the largest emission intensity was compared. The results showed that N, S-CDs-Sop had the highest emission intensity, followed by S-CDs-Sop and then CDs-Sop and N-CDs-Sop. However, the maximum emission intensity of N-CDs-Sop was lower than that of CDs-Sop. Fluorescence emission of N, S-CDs-Sop, CDs-Sop, S-CDs-Sop, and N-CDs-Sop under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp irradiation was visible in the inset of Fig. 1. As displayed in Fig. 1a, CDs-Sop represented green-yellow light emission. When concentrated sulfuric acid was used as the sulfur dopant, S-CDs-Sop showed yellow fluorescence emission. Meanwhile, it was hard to obverse the emission of N-CDs-Sop obviously, suggesting that ammonia as the dopant failed to increase the luminous intensity of N-CDs-Sop. Moreover, N, S-CDs-Sop with dl -homocystine exhibited great brighter green-yellow color emission compared with undoped CDs-Sop, indicating successful synthesis of N, S-CDs-Sop fluorescent probe. Additionally, the quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs-Sop, S-CDs-Sop, N, S-CDs-Sop and CDs-Sop was measured to demonstrate their fluorescence effectiveness, and the values were 0.38 %, 0.54 %, 1.20 %, 0.53 %, respectively.

From the results of quantum yield and fluorescence intensity measurements, it could be concluded that N, S co-doping could enhance fluorescence performance. Nitrogen and surfer doping could change the surface state and facilitate a higher yield of radiative recombination [31]. Moreover, according to the survey of Wang et al. [32], the introduction of S elements in the form of CS to the surface of N, SCDs contributes to a higher QY. S doping could improve the fluorescence emission slightly as well, while it was not as obvious as N, S co-doping. However, N doping failed to enhance the luminescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N, S-CDs-Sop and CDs-Sop

ce:section.ce:para: The XPS analysis was carried out to characterize the elemental composition and chemical structure of N, S-CDs-Sop and CDs-Sop. In the full XPS spectra of N, S-CDs-Sop (Fig. 2 A), four typical peaks of C 1 s (284.6 eV), N 1 s (399.8 eV), O 1 s (530.9 eV), S 2p (163.2 eV) were observed. Moreover, Fig. 3 A showed the full XPS spectra of CDs-Sop, demonstrating prominent peaks appeared at 284.7, 531.0 and 399.7 eV which correspond to C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s, respectively. By comparing the full XPS spectra of N, S-CDs-Sop and CDs-Sop, the existence of the S 2p peak indicated that the successful incorporation of sulfur element into N, S-CDs-Sop. Further evidence that the doped S atoms existed in the N, S-CDs-Sop as the sulfide form rather than as the sulfate/sulfite form was provided by the S 2p peak appearing at approximately 163 eV but not at 168 eV [33].

The specific chemical structures and possible surface states were further characterized by high-resolution XPS spectra. The high-resolution C 1 s spectra of N, S-CDs-Sop and CDs-Sop (Fig. 2B and Fig. 3B) both displayed three characteristic peaks at around 284.6, 285.8 and 287.8 eV, which were associated with CC/CC, CS/CN/CO and CO [34]. The O 1 s spectra (Fig. 2C and Fig. 3C) could be separated into two peaks about at 530.8 and 531.9 eV attributed to CO and CO respectively [35]. The N 1 s spectra (Fig. 2D and Fig. 3D) displayed two nitrogen states of CN (399.8 eV) and NH (401.5 eV) [36]. The S 2p spectra (Fig. 2E) were composed of two peaks at 163.1 and 164.3 eV, corresponding to S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 [37]. As for CDs-Sop, the peak position in C 1 s, O1s, N 1 s spectra (Fig. 3B-D) and the attribution of chemical bonds were essentially the same as for N, S-CDs-Sop.

As demonstrated in Fig. 4 , the FTIR spectra revealed the existence of nearly identical functional groups of N, S-CDs-Sop and CDs-Sop, but still presented differences. This occurred as a result that N, S-CDs-Sop was doped by dl -homocystine on the basis of CDs-Sop. The characteristic bands around 3382 and 3213 cm−1 were mostly assigned to the OH and NH stretching vibration [34], indicating the presence of hydroxyl and amino groups, which was possibly related to their highly hydrophilic nature. The bands observed at 2932 and 2147 cm−1 could be attributed to CH and CC bonds [38]. The band at 1595 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO and CC bonds [39], and the band at 1400 cm−1 could be recognized as CN, NH and − COO groups [40]. Furthermore, the band at 1074 cm−1 could be ascribed to CO stretching vibration [36], and the band at 539 cm−1 probably was due to the stretching vibration of the SS bond [41].

The size distribution and morphology of N, S-CDs-Sop and CDs-Sop were evaluated and compared by TEM. As shown in Fig. 5 A and 5B, the N, S-CDs-Sop and CDs-Sop were nearly spherical and homogeneously dispersed in sight of TEM images. The diameter distribution histograms were obtained by calculating from around 100 particles selected randomly. It was discovered that the size distribution of N, S-CDs-Sop was 1.4–3.6 nm with an average size of 2.4 nm (Inset of Fig. 5A), and CDs-Sop had a size distribution ranging from 1.0 to 3.2 nm with an average size of 1.9 nm (Inset of Fig. 5B). Moreover, the particle distribution of CDs-Sop was less monodispersed than that of N, S-CDs-Sop, which was possibly due to the strong attraction between individual CDs-Sop.

Besides, DLS results characterized the zeta potentials of N, S-CDs-Sop and CDs-Sop. As displayed in Fig. 5C and 5D, both N, S-CDs-Sop and CDs-Sop exhibited the virtually same zeta potential with a nearly neutral charge, demonstrating that nitrogen and sulfur doping scarcely influenced the potential results.

The optical properties of N, S-CDs-Sop were characterized by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. As depicted in Fig. 6 A, the UV–vis spectrum (blue curve) of the N, S-CDs-Sop exhibited an apparent absorption band at 250 nm. That was mainly attributed to π-π* transitions resulting from the sp2 hybrid of the CC bond in the core of N, S-CDs-Sop [42]. The N, S-CDs-Sop not only had excellent UV–vis absorption capacity, but also displayed exceptional fluorescence performance. The maximum emission peak of N, S-CDs-Sop appeared at 425 nm when excited at 335 nm (red curve and black curve). Insets of Fig. 6A showed that the pale brown N, S-CDs-Sop solution was observed to exhibit a bright green-yellow color when exposed to 365 nm UV light.

Fig. 6B displayed the fluorescence emission spectra of N, S-CDs-Sop at different excitation wavelengths. When the excitation wavelength varied from 300 to 380 nm, the maximum emission wavelength of N, S-CDs-Sop shifted correspondingly from 415 to 455 nm. This phenomenon confirmed the typical excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior of CDs, which was consistent with biomass-based carbon dots reported before. This was primarily ascribed to the presence of different particle sizes, diverse surface defect states and multiple functional groups of N,S-CDs-Sop [43,44].

The stability of the fluorescent probe was a crucial consideration for practical application. Thus, the influence of NaCl concentration, temperature, and pH value on fluorescence intensity was evaluated by varying the external circumstances. Firstly, the impact of NaCl concentration was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 7 A and 7B, the fluorescence intensity remained almost constant as the NaCl concentration increased from 0.05 to 1.00 M. Then, the influence of temperature was tested in the range of 20-80℃. As shown in Fig. 7C and 7D, the fluorescence intensity slightly declined accordingly but the overall effect was not immediately apparent as the temperature rose.

The above results indicated that N, S-CDs-Sop displayed good tolerance to environments with high temperature and salt concentration. Furthermore, the effect of pH on fluorescence stability was investigated in the range of 2–13 (Fig. 7E). As the pH value varied between 2 and 7, the fluorescence emission barely changed. It was interesting to note that the fluorescence intensity under alkaline conditions was higher than that under neutrality. This might be attributed to the enhanced electron donating ability of hydrophilic groups (–OH, −NH2, –COOH) under alkaline conditions and the induced intermolecular hydrogen bond interaction between groups, finally resulting in the fluorescence enhancement [45,46].
To sum up, the green synthesized N, S-CDs-Sop with dl -homocystine doped demonstrated outstanding stability in most circumstances, providing favorable conditions for practical application.

With superior water solubility and optical stability, CDs have been extensively applied in the field of microscale sensing such as heavy metal ions or chemically active molecules. As literature had reported that metal ions and anions might effectively quench the fluorescence of CDs, the effect of certain common metal ions and anions on the fluorescence of N, S-CDs-Sop was explored [39].

Various metal ions and anions with the same concentration of 100 μM, including Ba2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, K+, Al3+, Mg2+, Na+, Pb2+, Sr2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, SO4 2-, CO3 2– and NO3 –, were separately introduced into N, S-CDs-Sop solution to investigate their quenching ability. As shown in Fig. 8 A and 8B, the fluorescence was obviously quenched after the addition of Fe3+, while the addition of the other ions hardly affected it. In addition, the impact of Fe3+ on N-CDs-Sop, S-CDs-Sop and CDs-Sop was explored as shown in Fig. S1. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of Fe3+ towards N, S-CDs-Sop was stronger than that towards the other three CDs. The result confirmed the excellent quenching performance of Fe3+ to N, S-CDs-Sop, providing the basis for the further experiment.

Fig. 9 reveals that the introduction of Fe3+ effectively quenched the fluorescence of N, S-CDs-Sop. With the concentration of Fe3+ increased from 5 to 100 μM, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs-Sop at 425 nm decreased when excited at 335 nm. At 25 μM, the fluorescence intensity has decreased by 60 %.

Interestingly, the quenched fluorescence of the N, S-CDs-Sop/Fe3+ system was recovered after the introduction of AMX. Owing to its advantageous chemical structure, AMX would coordinate with Fe3+, forming a strong chelate. The fluorescence was quickly recovered, thus providing an “on–off-on” strategy for AMX detection. Fig. 10 A shows that the presence of Fe3+ significantly reduced the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs-Sop while the introduction of AMX recovered the quenched fluorescence. The fluorescence recovery rate even increased to 90 % when the concentration of AMX was 100 μM. Fig. 10B displays the emission spectra of N, S-CDs-Sop/Fe3+ system with the addition of AMX ranging from 5 to 100 μM, indicating that the fluorescence intensity was enhanced accordingly as the concentration increased. Fig. 10C shows the fluorescence recovery trend of the N, S-CDs-Sop/Fe3+ system. As demonstrated in Fig. 10D, a linear relation was established between the fluorescence intensity (F-F0)/F0 and AMX concentration in the range of 5 to 80 μM, and the regression equation was (F-F0)/F0 = 0.00358 + 0.01176 × with a correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.99, where F and F0 represented the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm in the presence and absence of AMX, and × was AMX concentration. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 3.40 μM according to the formula LOD = 3 σ/s, where σ represented the blank standard deviation (n = 10) and s was the slope of the linear regression equation. In addition, the pH value was 7 at which the N, S-CDs-Sop/Fe3+ system was applied to detecting AMX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching and recovery mechanisms of fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR and XPS analysis results revealed that –OH, –NH2 and –COOH groups were enriched on the surface of the N, S-CDs-Sop. As a transition metal, Fe3+ readily combined with amino and hydroxyl groups, leading to the aggregation of adjacent CDs and the development of the N, S-CDs-Sop-Fe3+ complex. The quenching mechanism of fluorescence could be attributed to static quenching induced by the formation of ground-state complexes and dynamic quenching depending on diffusive collisions [47]. As observed in the UV–vis spectra of Fig. 11 A, an obvious absorption peak was developed at 295 nm after Fe3+ was added to N, S-CDs-Sop solution, which was caused by the creation of the N, S-CDs-Sop-Fe3+ chelate. Particularly, it was distinctly noticed that that a large number of aggregations were produced when an extremely high concentration (500 μM) of Fe3+ was introduced into the N, S-CDs-Sop solution, further confirming the potent interaction between Fe3+ and N, S-CDs-Sop.

To further demonstrate the quenching mechanism, the time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra of N, S-CDs-Sop with a specific concentration of Fe3+ (25 μM) were characterized to investigate the interaction between N, S-CDs-Sop and Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 11B, the average fluorescence lifetime of pure N, S-CDs-Sop was determined to be 5.16 ns according to three exponentials fitted from the decay curve. Moreover, the lifetime of N, S-CDs-Sop/Fe3+ system was 5.04 ns, indicating that the addition of Fe3+ had no discernible impact on the average fluorescence lifetime of N, S-CDs-Sop. This suggested that there was no electron transport between N, S-CDs-Sop and Fe3+ and the fluorescence quenching mechanism was static quenching. Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime with AMX presented in the N, S-CDs-Sop/Fe3+ system was 5.10 ns.

As a transition metal ion, Fe3+ possesses a wide variety of coordination properties and reactivity [48]. Meanwhile, AMX, a kind of β- lactam antibiotic, owned multiple potential combination sites. There have been experimental results demonstrating that AMX could interact with Fe3+ forming a tri-dentate chelate via NH2, NH, and oxygen of carbonyl β-lactam groups (Fig. 11C) [49]. Besides, it was discovered that the binding constant/association constant (K) of AMX and Fe3+ was 7.65 × 102 L·mol−1 [48]. Hence, it might be inferred that AMX could bind tightly with Fe3+ when the two co-existed in solution, leading to the recovery of fluorescence.

In addition to sensitivity, selectivity is also an important factor for assessing the detection effectiveness of fluorescent probes. To evaluate the selectivity for AMX, the effect of typical interfering substances, such as GSH, Ala, Met, Glu, EM, SD, TAP, Glucose, PG, GM, Try, TOB, Dap, His and TC, on N, S-CDs-Sop/Fe3+ system was investigated. The above interferents were added into N, S-CDs-Sop/Fe3+ system with the final concentration of 100 μM, and the results were depicted in Fig. 12 . Besides, the effect of some interferents that could react easily with Fe3+ such as bilirubin, fluoride ions, ascorbic acid, l -cysteine was explored as shown in Fig. S2. As a result, AMX was found to be capable of recovering the fluorescence quenched by Fe3+, suggesting that the system exhibited good selectivity for AMX detection and provide a possibility to be applied in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AMX in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the practical application of the N, S-CDs-Sop/Fe3+ detection system, the biological sample of human plasma was prepared to assess the feasibility research. The human plasma sample was simply pretreated according to Section 2.7, and then spiked with AMX at a series of concentrations. As displayed in Fig. 13 A, the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CDs-Sop/Fe3+ system at an emission wavelength of 425 nm (excitationat335 nm)increased as the concentration grew from 10 to 90 μM. The relationship between (F-F0)/F0 and AMX concentration was depicted in Fig. 13B. A linear regression equation illustrated in Fig. 13C was well established between the fluorescence intensity (F-F0)/F0 and AMX concentration in the range of 10–70 μM as follows: (F-F0)/F0 = -0.01008 + 0.01469 × (R2 = 0.99). The LOD was determined to be 3.46 μM. The result suggested that the proposed analysis method was reliable and applicable for detecting AMX in actual samples.

Table 1 exhibited a series of methods for AMX detection by comparing linear range and LOD. It was found that our article provided a simple, sensitive and reliable sensing strategy without the necessity for expensive apparatus or additional pre-treatment.

In this work, a facile N, S-CDs-Sop derived from S.japonica flower (main carbon source) and dl -homocystine (nitrogen and sulfur source) was synthesized successfully through a one-step hydrothermal process. The obtained N, S-CDs-Sop had good water solubility and excellent fluorescence properties. It displayed a vivid green-yellow color under UV irradiation and functioned as the sensitive fluorescence sensor for AMX detection through an “off–on” strategy. Additionally, this novel sensing system realized AMX detection in human plasma samples successfully. Thus, a simple, sensitive, and rapid fluorescence sensing strategy for AMX was effectively established, exhibiting the application potential in complicated biological samples.

Xin-Ran Guo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yu-Ming Dong: Methodology, Investigation. Xin-Yue Chen: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Juan Chen: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors greatly appreciate the financial support from the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (NO. 2019M663855), the Special Foundation for National Science and Technology Basic Resources of China (No. 2018FY100701), the Key Program of Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province, China (No. 20JR10RA584) and the 2021 project of Gansu Medical Products Administration (No. 2021GSMPA008).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121703.

ce:section.ce:para:
